text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Day 11 ofParis 2024was a slower one in terms of medals than we’ve become accustomed to fromTeam GBover the last week, but a pair of silvers arrived by Tuesday evening.

The first came courtesy of the British men’s team pursuit squad, with Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin beaten in a gold medal race by new world record holders the Netherlands, while GB’s second silver medal of the day was won less than an hour later by Josh Kerr in the 1500m final.

Kerr, the current world champion in the event, looked destined for gold but was overtaken at the end by the USA’s Cole Hocker while defending Olympic gold medallistJakob Ingebrigtsenmissed the podium entirely.

That followed a bronze medal for 16-year-old Sky Brown in the women’s park skateboarding final, who replicated her podium finish from Tokyo three years ago despite competing with a dislocated shoulder. Meanwhile in Marseille, Micky Beckett must wait until at least tomorrow to contest for a medal of his own after the men’s dinghy final was delayed due to low winds.

Elsewhere at the Stade de France tonight, Team GB sprint stars Dina Asher-Smith andDaryll Neitafinished just shy of the podium in the women’s 200m final, placing fourth and fifth respectively.

This live blog has now closed.

Thanks for joining us as Team GB added three medals totheir tally.

It’s just a shame none of them were of the gold variety.

You can readJames Gray’s report from Stade de France here.

We’ll be back bright and early tomorrow to do it all over again.

Bye for now.

Lewis Richardson had to settle for bronze after being beaten on split decision by Mexico’s Marco Verde in the men’s 71kg semi-final at Roland Garros.

Richardson started well landing a solid punch to Verde’s face, but the Mexican grew back into the round throwing a series of different combinations and breaking through the British fighter’s defence.

The second round saw the British fighter show his skills off in the following round, landing a great uppercut and a couple of big blows to Verde’s head to take the round.

An evenly-poised contest boiled down to the third round and Richardson began to use his reach more, throwing a big strike to Verde’s nose.

The Mexican caught the British fighter on the cheek but struggled to land some big swings before being hit in the face again by Richardson.

STADE DE FRANCE– Close but no cigar for Team GB, as Dina Asher-Smith finishes fourth, edging Daryll Neita into fifth.

USA’s Gabrielle Thomas the class of the field ahead of 100m champ Julien Alfred and fellow American Brittany Brown.

For what it’s worth, that was the top three I predicted, witnessed by the bloke sitting next to me, who has now bolted to the mixed zone so I have no verification.

So close, but not quite enough for a medal from the two Brits in the 200m final.

A photo-finish sees Dina Asher-Smith place fourth with a 22.22, with Daryll Neita (22.23) just behind her in fifth.

Final results:

STADE DE FRANCE– Got your breath back? Not sure I have. Here comes the final of the 200m.

Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita go for Great Britain. Tall order for them in this company, but heh, Cole Hocker set the shocker template so who knows?

Elsewhere at the Stade de France tonight, Jacob Fincham-Dukes leapt to a fifth-placed finish in the men’s long jump final to end anunderwhelming run for Team GB in the field events so far.

STADE DE FRANCE– What a finish in the 3,000m steeplechase. Defending champion Peruth Chemutai is utterly distraught while Winfred Yavi of Bahrain is dancing on the track after a stunning finish that saw her overtake the Ugandan in the last 50 metres.

Amazing noise in the Stade de France as Yavi breaks the Olympic record too.

Great run from Britain’s Elizabeth Bird as well. She breaks her own national record to finish seventh in 9:04.35.

Want some more stats from that dramatic 1500m final?

The top four – Cole Hocker, Josh Kerr, Yared Nuguse and Jakob Ingebrigtsen – wereallfaster than the Olympic record of 3:28.32 set by Ingebrigtsen when he won gold in Tokyo three years ago.

What a race, gents. Personal best times for the top three as well.

STADE DE FRANCE– Josh Kerr breaks his personal best in that 1500m by more than a second, but it’s only a silver medal.

My word.

10th place in the final for GB’s Neil Gourley.

STADE DE FRANCE– Kerr moved up to second with 250m to go, then hit the front with 50m to go… but THERE GOES HOCKER!

COLE HOCKER HAS WON IT FOR THE USA!

Josh Kerr takes silver in a photo-finish and Jakob Ingebrigtsen is run out of the medals.

No one saw that coming!

A new Olympic record and only a second off the world record for Hocker.

Wow. WOW! It’s the USA’s Cole Hocker who wins gold!

Josh Kerr takes silver for GB and there is no medal for defending champion Jakob Ingebrigtsen.

Bronze goes to another American, Yared Nuguse.

STADE DE FRANCE– Ingebrigtsen leads with a lap to go. World record isn’t far off this pace either….

STADE DE FRANCE– Ingebrigtsen is going to lead it out and make Kerr hurt for this.

54 seconds for the first lap. Kerr sitting third, tracking every move.

STADE DE FRANCE– Now for one of the showdowns of the Games. GB’s Josh Kerr against Norway’s Jakob Ingebrigtsen in the 1500m. It’s the Ali-Frazier of middle-distance running.

Huge cheers for Kerr when his name is read out. There are a lot of Brits in here tonight. Can the Scot deliver for them? Three and three quarter laps of the track will decide it.

The finalists are coming onto the track for this men’s 1500m.

There are two Brits in this race: Neil Gourley andJosh Kerr.

The latter, alongsidefierce rival and defending champ Jakob Ingebrigtsen of Norway, is expected to be a favourite for the gold medal.

Kerr is the reigning world champion in this event – can he add Olympic champion to his CV as well?

Keely Hodgkinson’s gold in the women’s 800m finalhighlighted the athletics action for Team GB last night, and ahead of today’s finals session she picked up her medal.

Here are a few images from that moment.

STADE DE FRANCE– Oh no! GB’s Lina Nielsen, going for a place in the 400m hurdles final, clatters the final barrier and hits the deck.

She is up and walking to the finish line, but that is a sad finish. She was on for third and would have needed a fastest loser’s spot, but would have had a chance.

Norway’s Amalie Ieul is at the finish line to give her a hug but Nielsen is absolutely gutted.

Looking at it, and she won’t like this, but I think Nielsen was on for a massive PB. She may have beaten the South African to the line and she clocked 53.90. Nielsen’s previous best was 54.43. So cruel.

Bonus fact: Nielsen’s twin sister Laviai is running the 400m for Great Britain here.

It’s a silver medal for the GB men’s team sprint squad of Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin!

The Netherlands take the gold, having set a new world record for the second time today.

It means the Dutch group of Harrie Lavreysen, Roy van den Berg and Jeffrey Hoogland have defended their Olympic title, three years after defeating a British trio in the final of this event in Tokyo.

World records have beentumblingat the Velodrome, with the British womensetting new all-time marks three times yesterday en route to their team sprint gold.

The world record was broken a total of five times in that event on Monday, and today there have been two world records in the men’s team sprint (both from the Netherlands, who had previously set a new Olympic record in the event yesterday) and another in the men’s team pursuit, courtesy of Australia.

STADE DE FRANCE– Wow. Quite the statement from GB’s Matt Hudson-Smith.

Fastest man in the world this year, gapped the field in the first 300m and then eased down the last 50m to clock 44.07. Was wagging his finger as he crossed the line and clearly has plenty in the tank.

See you back here at 8.20pm UK time tomorrow night, when he runs for Olympic gold.

STADE DE FRANCE– So true Kevin, and note the Zambian Muzala Samukonga: he’s only 21, two years ago was the Commonwealth champion, and has just run a national record of 43.81 right behind James.

Both threats for medals in the final tomorrow, in my humble opinion. Samukonga a massive improver.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Asher-Smith pipped to Olympic bronze after Kerr wins silver for Team GB
author: Alex Dakers, George Simms, Tom Ward
description: Follow's i's live blog from day 11 of the Olympic Games in Paris
